text: Muscle glycogenolysis and rates of carbohydrate oxidation are regulated by exercise intensity, duration, training status, and substrate availability.
High carbohydrate availability before, during, and after high-intensity training sessions or competition promotes endurance exercise performance.
Muscle glycogen serves not only as a fuel store but also as a potent regulator of molecular cell signaling pathways that control the oxidative phenotype.
Deliberately training with low carbohydrate availability can enhance oxidative training adaptations by upregulating cell signaling pathways associated with the oxidative phenotype.
Glycogen metabolism during exercise is regulated through both hormonal control and local allosteric regulation of key enzymes and transport proteins.

A periodized approach to carbohydrate availability — high availability for key sessions and competition, low availability for selected training sessions — may optimize both performance and training adaptations.
